Note: This module is not tested on the exam, except the first section on how your body
produces sex hormones, but we've still provided learning objectives and a mock exam
to enhance the learning experience for those interested in this module.

When most people talk about steroids, they're actually referring to performance
enhancing drugs (PEDs) or anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS). Vitamin D, for
example, is also a steroid hormone, as are various medications without any muscle
building effects. There are also several other PEDs used by bodybuilders with the goal
to increase muscle growth or lose fat, such as growth hormone, insulin and thyroid
hormone. That most people don't even properly know what the terms mean illustrates
the lack of scientific knowledge on the topic. In this module of the course, we'll ignore
the taboo and discuss in scientific terms what 'steroids' do to the human body. This
topic is not about teaching you how to use steroids but rather inform you about the risks
and benefits from a medical point of view so that you can properly inform your clients
and potentially help mitigate the risks.

➢ Lecture
The costs and benefits of using androgenic-anabolic steroids (AAS)

➢ Case study
Deciding whether or not to use AAS
headings: topic is not about teaching you how to use steroids but rather inform you about the risks

pages.text:
4

How your body produces sex hormones
Before looking at the effects of supplementing exogenous steroids, it helps to
understand how the human body produces its own steroids and what they do. Sex
hormone production differs considerably between the sexes and we'll look at men first,
because it's easy to understand how it works in women once you understand the
process in men.

Men
The hypothalamus in our brain is basically the master regulator of hormone levels. It
releases gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), which stimulates the pituitary gland
in the brain to release luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle stimulating hormone (FSH).
LH increases steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) in the Leydig cells of the
testicles. The testicles then start producing androgens, literally 'man making hormones',
notably testosterone. Almost all (95%) testosterone in men is produced by the testes,
but the adrenal glands also contribute about 5%. Steroids are produced from
cholesterol, so cholesterol is the fundamental building block of all sex hormones.
FSH also stimulates the testes but to produce sperm rather than testosterone.

Estrogen production in men occurs primarily via aromatization (conversion) of
androgens in adipose tissue (body fat) but also in bone and the testes. Testosterone is
converted to estradiol and androstenedione to estrone.
headings: How your body produces sex hormones

pages.text:
5

Steroid production in the male body.

The testes produce 4 - 9 mg of testosterone per day in the average healthy male.

Male testosterone production is controlled by a negative feedback loop that measures
the level of androgens and estrogens in the body. This needs to be at a certain level.
When that level is reached, the hypothalamus stops stimulating the pituitary to release
LH and FSH, which are then no longer there to activate the gonads, which then stop
producing testosterone and sperm. This testosterone is converted to estrogen via the
aromatase enzyme, primarily in adipose tissue (body fat).

6

When a man is on exogenous (meaning 'from outside of the body') androgenic-anabolic
steroids (AAS), these are registered by the hypothalamus just as if they were the body's
own testosterone and thus natural production is shut down: there are already enough
androgens in circulation as far as the body goes, so further production isn't needed.
Natural production of testosterone is fully suppressed as soon as exogenous dosages
exceed physiological production levels, so in practice, you can assume any use of AAS
fully shuts down the testes.

The negative feedback loop regulation of testosterone production in men. Endogenous
testosterone and exogenous AAS have the same suppressive effect on the
hypothalamus and pituitary. Adapted from Source

The suppression of natural production is undesirable, as it results in infertility, atrophy of
the testes ('raisin nuts') and over time the possibility of complete cell death. This would

7

render someone infertile and hypogonadal, incapable of producing enough testosterone
and consequently estrogen for normal physiological functioning.

Women
Women produce only 5-10% as much testosterone as men, but androgens are
important for female functioning just like estrogens are important for male functioning.
Sex hormone production in women starts in the brain just like in men. The
hypothalamus produces GnRH, which stimulates the pituitary to release LH and FSH.

Since women don't have testicles, things obviously differ from here on out. LH binds not
to Leydig cells in the testes but to Theca cells in the ovaries, which then produce
androgens. Whereas men are practically entirely reliant on their testes to produce
testosterone, women derive only about half of their testosterone from the ovaries. The
other half comes indirectly from the adrenal gland, which lies on top of your kidneys.
The adrenals are also under control of the pituitary via release of adrenocorticotropic
hormone (ACTH) and together form the hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. The
adrenal cortex produces androgen precursor hormones, namely DHEA sulfate
(DHEAS), DHEA and androstenedione. These weaker androgens are converted to
testosterone in their target tissues.

8

Sex hormone production in the adrenal gland. Source

Women produce estrogen via aromatization of testosterone in peripheral tissues,
particularly adipose tissue, just like in men, but the ovaries also directly produce a lot of
estrogen. In the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle, the corpus luteum also produces
estrogen directly.

9

Women do not have the same simple negative feedback loop of testosterone production
as men. There is negative feedback based on the level of estradiol and progesterone,
but it interacts with the menstrual cycle phase in a complex manner, illustrated below. In
practice, most women can use AAS without experiencing shutdown of natural sex
hormone production. They will often, but not always, lose their menstrual cycle, but
there is relatively little risk of long-term infertility and sex hormone deficiency.

Natural hormone production in women.

During pregnancy, sex hormone levels can increase majorly. Even testosterone levels
can increase 3- to 4-fold.

Menopause & HRT
Women in their 40s or 50s go into menopause, a state of ovarian senescence. The
process begins around age 35 with full menopause - the complete loss of menstruation
- occurring at an average age of 51. As the ovaries stop functioning, sex hormone
production and fertility decrease majorly. Most women become completely infertile,

10

testosterone levels drop by roughly half and estrogen levels decrease from 15-350
pg/mL (wide variation across the menstrual cycle) to below 10 pg/mL. Postmenopausal
estrogen levels are actually on average lower than those of adult men (10-40 pg/mL).
These dramatic decreases in sex hormone levels result in a wide range of side-effects,
including lower mental health and wellbeing, loss of libido and vaginal dryness, poorer
connective tissue strength and many more problems, as illustrated below.

Female hormone levels before and after menopause. Source

11

Symptoms of menopause. Source

Throughout most of human evolution, women would not live much past the age of
menopause, but in modern societies, women spend about a third of their lives post-
menopause. Therefore, women undergoing menopause are recommended to consider
hormone replacement therapy (HRT) to bring their hormone levels back to their
previous levels, undoing many of the effects of menopause.
• HRT improves women's body composition and physical performance without
exercise and augments muscle growth and some measures of physical
performance with exercise.

12

• HRT likely reduces injury risk during strength training, as menopause causes
bone loss and increased susceptibility to muscle damage. Estrogen stimulates
collagen synthesis in connective tissues, notably tendons. Estrogen also reduces
connective tissue stiffness, which can be good or bad, depending on the context.
Reducing estrogen levels with aromatase inhibitors frequently causes joint pain
as a side-effect and AAS users with low estrogen levels also frequently
experience joint pain, anecdotally.
• HRT significantly improves skin and hair quality.
• Aside from the physical benefits, HRT's main benefit is arguably decreased
anxiety, decreased depression and a better overall mood.

HRT does not affect all-cause mortality, although it may increase the risk of some
cardiovascular problems (without a net increase in mortality). The risk of
cardiovascular complications may primarily result from progestogens, because
estrogen-only HRT has been found to improve cardiovascular health [2]. Still, some
progesterone is usually incorporated into HRT to reduce the risk of endometrial
cancer.

➢ Recommended reading for postmenopausal women
IMS recommendations on women's midlife health and menopause hormone therapy

13

AAS mechanism of action
AAS operate primarily via genomic effects: they bind to the androgen receptor, which
activates genetic pathways to encode new proteins. This heightened rate of protein
synthesis alters your body structure and functions, including large increases in muscle
protein synthesis with rapid muscle growth and strength development [2]. Since this is
an indirect and slow process, the effects of AAS continue to accumulate for months
during their use. This goes for muscle growth as well as the side-effects. The images
below illustrate AAS' mechanism of action and their effects in the body.

How androgens improve muscular development. Source

14

Testosterone's effects in relation to that of strength training. Source

15

A list of the performance enhancing effects of androgens. Source

AAS also have non-genomic effects [2]: effects that are not mediated by your genes.
Instead of entering into a muscle cell and instructing it to increase muscle protein
synthesis, testosterone can also act on receptors and calcium channels on the cell
membrane. These effects are far more rapid and direct, but they contribute considerably
less to neuromuscular development than the genomic effects. The non-genomic effects
seem to be mediated mostly via membrane receptors that affect calcium metabolism. In
muscle fibers, the presence of calcium triggers the reactions that make actin and
myosin form cross-bridges, which create muscle contractions. Altered calcium
metabolism appears to be the primary non-genomic mechanism by which AAS increase
muscle strength almost acutely.

Dose-response effects
The effect of AAS are strongly dose-dependent: the more AAS you have in your blood,
the greater the androgenic and anabolic effects and thus the more muscle you'll gain [2,
3]. This does-response effect is illustrated in the graph below.

16

The more testosterone you give someone, the more muscle they'll gain. Source

Since the side-effects of AAS are also dose-dependent, many novice AAS users think
they can get away with 'using just a little bit', but this In the figure you can see there is
no positive effect on muscle growth until you give a man more than 50 mg per week of
testosterone. Doses below 100 mg/wk are essentially testosterone replacement therapy
(TRT). They do not increase your total blood testosterone levels. Because testosterone
levels are regulated via a negative feedback loop in men, up until this point you're just
replacing natural androgens with exogenous ones. Only when you take higher doses,
do you reach supraphysiological androgen levels and then you can achieve increased
muscle growth. Using a TRT-like dose this thus takes all the risks of shutting down your
natural production without anything to show for it. For an AAS cycle, any dose below
300 mg/wk is arguably futile.

Doses of up to 600 mg/wk of testosterone have been relatively well-documented [2, 3,
4] and do not cause high acute health hazards, although they certainly cause clinical
side-effects, especially in the elderly. There is scant research on higher doses, so 600

17

mg/wk may be considered the upper limit of medically responsible risk taking. In
contrast, many professional bodybuilders have been reported to take around 2000
mg/wk of AAS, in addition to various other PEDs.

Choice of AAS
Bro steroid lore bases much of its knowledge on which AAS to use on their androgenic-
anabolic ratios. The idea is that you want AAS with very anabolic effects but little
androgenic effects: maximum muscle growth with as little of the side-effects as possible.
However, the AA ratios commonly cited are based on research in rodents. We know
that in humans, AAS operate principally via the androgen receptor, which causes both
the anabolic and the androgenic effects. It's thus impossible for any AAS to have
anabolic effects without androgenic effects. The two go hand in hand. Accordingly,
research finds that on a mg per mg basis, most AAS increase lean body mass to the
same extent: see the figure below.

18

The dose-response effect of various AAS is very similar on a mg per mg basis. Source

Other research supports that trenbolone acetate, commonly abbreviated to tren, is no
more anabolic or androgenic than DHT or testosterone in human cells (but it does have
unique other effects not mediated by the AR), and 300 mg 19-nortestosterone
decanoate, commonly abbreviated to deca, even appears to be slightly weaker than 300
mg testosterone, in contrast to what you'd expect based on their AA ratios in rodents.
Furthermore, adding a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor to a testosterone cycle, which
decreases the level of DHT while increasing testosterone, does not affect gains in lean
body mass, indicating DHT and testosterone have similar anabolic effects.

19

The principal determinant of any AAS cycle or stack is thus simply the total weekly mg
of AAS, analogous to the effects of training volume on muscle growth (i.e. total sets per
muscle) and energy intake for fat loss (total kcal per week). With this in mind, the most
important consideration of which AAS to use should generally not be which is strongest
but which is safest. In this sense, testosterone should always be considered first,
because it's a natural substance for the human body and it's by far the most extensively
researched AAS. The popular saying in bodybuilding circles that 'test is best' rings true
in many cases. However, testosterone may not always be appropriate for an individual's
goals, so let's discuss a few notable factors that differentiate AAS. One major
determinant of the safety of an AAS is whether it's 17α-alkylated.

Orals vs. injectables
Raw hormones are not absorbed well by the body. They are typically broken down
before they make it to the bloodstream, in particular by the liver. Chemists have found a
way around this with a process called c-17α-alkylation in order to make AAS orally
consumable. All oral AAS, except some rather ineffective forms of oral testosterone,
mesterolone (commonly known under the brand name Proviron) and metenolone
acetate (commonly known under the brand name Primobolan) are 17α-alkylated,
whereas almost no injectable AAS are 17-alpha-alkylated. Stanozolol, commonly known
under the brand name Winstrol, is the only know-worthy exception of an AAS that is
commonly injected yet still is hepatotoxic and can be consumed orally as well. Making
the AAS resistant to the liver's attempts at breaking it down makes it orally effective, but
it also puts a major strain on the liver, resulting in many additional side-effects. Since
the liver plays a crucial role in human metabolism and detoxification of the body, the
side-effect profile of oral AAS is often dramatically worse than that of injectables at
higher doses. The worst cases of strange pathologies, hospitalizations and deaths from
AAS use almost always included oral AAS. Since there is very little orals can do that
injectables can't, oral AAS should generally be avoided by men. Women can achieve

20

significant gains from much lower doses of AAS, so orals are comparatively safer to use
for them up to a point.

Half-lives
Largely depending on which ester an AAS is bound to, AAS vary significantly in their
terminal half-lives: the time it takes for the body to eliminate half of the AAS from the
bloodstream after its peak. The half-life does not fundamentally change the effects of
the AAS, but having major fluctuations in blood concentrations of the AAS can increase
its side-effects, as all women with a menstrual cycle can attest to. Acne is particularly
common during large changes in blood AAS levels. The fluctuations arising from AAS
with different half-lives are illustrated below, as are the half-lives of common
testosterone preparations.

Source

21

Source

22

Source

To prevent excessive swings in AAS levels, a good rule of thumb is to use an injection
frequency at least equal to twice its half-life. For example, testosterone enanthate
should ideally be injected at least once per 4.5 × 2 = 9 days to keep blood levels
relatively stable.

Frontloading
Another strategy to prevent excessive fluctuations in AAS levels is frontloading. If you
look at the graph above of weekly testosterone enanthate injections, you'll see the
testosterone blood concentration accumulates slowly over time, because not all the
testosterone from the previous injection is eliminated by the time of the next dose. It
then takes around 5 weeks to reach peak testosterone concentration. That's 5 weeks of
constantly changing testosterone concentrations below the target level. Frontloading is
a method to get to target level from the first injection onward. Based on an AAS's half-
life, we can calculate how much AAS is needed for the initial injection to immediately
reach equilibrium levels. During the start of AAS usage or when changing the dose, the
following formula calculates how should be injected for the first dose to reach target
equilibrium blood concentration as rapidly as possible.

𝐹𝑟𝑜𝑛𝑡𝑙𝑜𝑎𝑑 (𝑚𝑔) =
𝐷
1 −2−𝑇/𝐻
D = dosage per injection in mg
T = time in days between injections
H = terminal half-life of the AAS in days

For example, 250 mg testosterone enanthate injected every other day requires a
frontload of 250 / (1 - 2^(-2/4.5)) = ~1000 mg on the first day. Here's a calculator that
does the math for you.

23

Aromatization
Another considerable difference between AAS is whether they aromatize - convert to
estrogen - in our body or not. Aromatization in men preserves the natural ratio of
testosterone to estrogen in the body. Estrogen helps counteract androgenic side-
effects, such as the decrease in HDL-cholesterol, and as you learned, estrogen itself
also has many desirable anti-catabolic and even anabolic effects, though they're not as
potent as testosterone's. However, estrogen also causes considerable sodium and
water retention. Trainees on high doses of testosterone will often experience edema
and a puffy appearance including 'moon face', so they will not see much muscle
definition, especially in the abdominal region, as it's covered by a thick layer of
subcutaneous fluid. A significant portion of the increases in lean body mass during a
testosterone cycle - many kilos in the case of a heavyweight bodybuilder - are
essentially just water, which explains why strength does not increase nearly as much as
lean body mass during a testosterone cycle.

To counteract this aesthetic problem, bodybuilders often use non-aromatizing AAS so
that they get more muscular without getting bloated. The last month(s) before a non-
natural bodybuilding show, non-aromatizing AAS are essentially a must to look lean on
stage. The use of aromatizing vs. non-aromatizing AAS thus presents an inevitable
compromise between aesthetics and androgenic side-effects.

AAS for women
The myth of androgenic to anabolic ratios has given rise to the idea of AAS for women
in bro bodybuilding culture. The truth is, there is no such thing as an AAS that will not
cause virilizing side-effects in women. Oxandrolone is popular with women because it
does not aromatize, but the only reason it's known as less virilizing is because it is

24

typically used in low doses, as it's generally incredibly expensive. Physiologically,
however, 'test is best' likely also applies to women, because virtually all of estrogen's
effects are desirable in women, except often its water retention. The main problem with
testosterone use in women is that it's difficult to micro-dose. Most testosterone
preparations are made with men in mind, so the doses are far too high for women.
Moderate AAS use for women that should not cause major virilization is around 35-105
mg per week (5-15 mg per day). Anything over 350 mg per week (50 mg per day) is
hard to tolerate for more than a few weeks or months without noticeable virilization. The
typically highest tolerable dose in women is thus around the lowest effective dose for a
cycle in men.

An unpopular but anecdotally effective alternative to testosterone in women is
oxymetholone. Oxymetholone causes estrogenic side-effects such as gynecomastia
and major water retention, suggesting it has direct estrogenic effects despite not
aromatizing. Like virtually all oral AAS, it will strain the liver, but even 100 mg per day
for 3 months has been reported to be reasonably well-tolerated in many women. The
estrogenic activity may offset some of the androgenic side-effects.

SARMs
Selective androgen receptor modulators (SARMs) are supposed to be the future of
steroids. Their intent is to activate the androgen receptor like AAS do without being
susceptible to human enzymes. Without any metabolic byproducts, the SARM should
have fewer side-effects than AAS and not affect your natural anabolic hormone
production as much.

One of the few relevant studies we have on SARMs is Dalton et al. (2011). The
researchers investigated the effects of GTx-024 AKA enobosarm AKA ostarine AKA
MK-2866 in healthy elderly men and post-menopausal women. After 12 weeks, the
results as presented in the abstract seem promising: "GTx-024 treatment resulted in
dose-dependent increases in total lean body mass that were statistically significant and

25

clinically meaningful. There were also significant improvements in physical function
(P = 0.013, 3 mg vs. placebo) and insulin resistance. The incidence of adverse events
was similar between treatment groups." The authors worked for the company GTx that
produces SARMs, however, and a closer inspection reveals more sobering findings.

While the authors present an impressive graph showing relative changes in lean body
mass (see below), only the highest dosage of 3 mg per day resulted in a significant
increase in lean body mass and this was a mere 1.3 kg. They were also the only group
to lose a significant amount of fat, but this was only 0.6 kg.

Lack of gains is one thing, but the side-effects the authors conveniently omitted in the
abstract were serious.
• 'Good' HDL cholesterol decreased in dose-response fashion with a considerable
28% drop in the highest dosage group, i.e. the more of the SARM they took, the
worse their cholesterol levels got.
• Natural hormone production was negatively affected. Testosterone levels in the
men decreased in dose-response fashion with a 43% decrease in the highest
dosage group. Interestingly, even the women showed impaired hormone
functioning. The highest dosage group experienced a decrease in FSH and LH

26

levels. All groups experienced a dose-response decrease in estradiol production
with a 34% decrease in the highest dosage group.

It's also worth noting one subject had to be removed from the study because his ALT
liver enzyme values were too high. 21% of subjects in the highest dosage group
experienced an increase in ALT, suggesting liver damage.

The only other relevant SARM study we have is Basaria et al. (2013). This is again an
industry sponsored study. 76 Healthy men took 0.1, 0.3 or 1.0 mg per day of the SARM
LGD-4033 for 21 days. The results are similar to those of Dalton et al. There was a
dose-response increase in lean body mass of 1.2 kg in the highest dosage group and a
concurrent decrease in testosterone and HDL levels. At the highest dosage, FSH and
free testosterone were also negatively affected. Interestingly, the SARM did not affect
strength or physical performance markers. Fasting muscle protein synthesis levels also
did not increase. These findings could mean the increase in lean body mass was mostly
water or other tissue than muscle, similar to growth hormone.

So are SARMs the magic pill? The data we have indicate they function very similarly to
(oral) AAS. Even at very modest dosages the side-effects are already significant; for
women the side-effects are even worse than AAS it seems. Research in rats found that
the SARM S-23 decreased natural testosterone production so much it's of better use as
a male contraceptive than a doping drug. There's no telling what havoc SARMs will
wreak on your body at dosages that are comparably potent to an AAS cycle.

Moreover, SARMs also suffer the same problem as AAS: if you buy SARMs from a non-
pharmaceutical grade source, there is a good chance the product is impure.

In short, while the theory behind SARMs is nice, there's little research to support they
actually work and no evidence that they cause fewer side-effects than AAS at

27

comparably effective dosages. The use of SARMs cannot be considered medically
responsible at this point.

28

Side-effects of AAS cycles
AAS use comes with multiple well-established side-effects [2]. Virtually no AAS users
are free from side-effects. Below we will discuss the most common and notable side-
effects.

Hypogonadism
As discussed, the use of external androgens will suppress natural androgen production
in men, resulting in hypogonadism. Without the use of ancillaries to mitigate the
hypogonadism and improve its recovery, it typically takes months for the testes to fully
recover after AAS use and natural testosterone production may still not reach pre-use
levels. To prevent hypogonadism during AAS use, hCG can and generally should be
used.

hCG to prevent testicular shutdown
Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is a hormone produced by a woman's placenta
during pregnancy. It's measured by many urinary pregnancy tests. As it turns out, hCG
is an analog of LH, so injecting hCG in men stimulates the testes to produce
testosterone just like LH. As such, hCG use maintains fertility and testicular functioning
during AAS use [2, 3]. However, since hCG does not mimic the effects of FSH, which is
needed for maximum spermatogenesis, hCG maintains the quality but not the full
quantity of sperm on-cycle. hCG also stimulates the production of all androgens,
estradiol and progesterone.

In contrast to conventional practice, which is based on women, the dosage requirement
for men is very low. It takes an injection dosage of only 250-500 IUs hCG every other
day to fully maintain testicular functioning. 500 IU twice a week comes close to fully
maintaining testicular functioning as well. Higher dosages mainly stimulate more

29

estradiol production with little additional effect on testicular functioning [2]: see the
graphs below.

The dose-response relation between hCG and testosterone and estradiol levels.

The following graphic illustrates how hCG use circumvents testosterone's negative
feedback loop and keeps the testes active on-cycle.

30

How hCG and SERMs affect hormonal and testicular functioning. Adapted from Source

Take-home message: hCG should be injected by men on-cycle at a dosage of 250-500
IUs every other day while natural production is suppressed and until recovery can take
place. Common dosage protocols are 300 IU every other day or 500 IU twice per week.
hCG can be injected either subcutaneously or intramuscularly, typically with an insulin
needle.

Post-cycle therapy
In addition to the use of hCG on-cycle to maintain testicular functioning, the risks of
infertility and hypogonadism in men may be reduced with post-cycle therapy (PCT).
hCG should not be used during PCT, as it artificially maintains testicular functioning by
mimicking LH and in doing so still suppresses hypothalamic-pituitary functioning. It
keeps the testicles but not the brain healthy. Instead, after an AAS cycle, recovery of
the full HPG axis can be sped up with the consumption of selective estrogen receptor
modulators (SERMs) [2, 3, 4, 5].

31

SERMs, as the name suggests, modulate estrogen receptors. Depending on the tissue,
they either activate the estrogen receptor with subsequent estrogenic effects, including
a favorable increase in HDL cholesterol, or block the receptor with subsequent anti-
estrogenic effects. Blocking occurs in the hypothalamus, effectively rendering the
hypothalamus unable to see there is estrogen and thereby circumventing its negative
feedback loop to the pituitary and gonads. As a result, SERMs increase LH and FSH,
which in turn increase sperm, testosterone and estrogen production. Testosterone
levels typically increase around 50% with the use of a SERM at full potency.

Aromatase inhibitors (AIs) can also be used to increase testosterone levels by reducing
the conversion of testosterone to estrogen. However, this does not benefit
hypothalamic-pituitary functioning and reductions in estrogen are not desirable when
estrogen levels aren't excessively high in the first place. You're merely trading one sex
hormone deficiency for another without addressing the root cause.

How SERMs and AIs increase testosterone production. Source

32

The most effective and safe SERMs are clomiphene, common brand name Clomid, and
tamoxifen, common brand name Nolvadex. Both have a long, successful track record in
the treatment of hypogonadism. However, tamoxifen is preferable, as clomiphene has a
few more notable side-effects. Clomiphene can decrease the LH response to LH-
releasing hormone (LHRH), whereas tamoxifen doesn't. Clomiphene also has the very
rare but notable side-effect of night-blindness.

High dosages of SERMs are often used with the idea that more is better, but they're not
necessary and needlessly risky. 50 mg clomiphene per day is no more effective than 25
mg and 20 mg tamoxifen is comparably effective to 150 mg clomiphene. So 25 mg of
clomiphene or 20 mg of tamoxifen is enough for maximum effect.

Due to the difference in sex hormone production, SERMs are not effective at increasing
sex hormone production in women.

Take-home message: Tamoxifen should be consumed at 20 mg per day post-cycle from
the time natural production can begin to restore until it has restored. Recovery should
take only a few weeks this way.

Cardiovascular side-effects
AAS have various side-effects in the cardiovascular system. The following are the most
common and notable adverse effects.
• Decreased HDL-cholesterol and often also increased LDL-cholesterol. This
worsening of the cholesterol profile is an almost inevitable side-effect of AAS
use, because androgens directly stimulate hepatic lipase, a liver enzyme
responsible for breaking down HDL-cholesterol. The poorer cholesterol profile
can be successfully counteracted by an overall healthy lifestyle, diet and body
composition to an extent. For severe cases, statins could be considered, but

33

statins have muscular side-effects, so it's questionable if that will achieve the
desired outcomes for an AAS user.
• Elevated blood pressure. Hypertension can be very effectively and safely
managed using various drugs. For hypertensive athletes, the first-line options to
consider are thiazide diuretics and angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE)
inhibitors.
• A rise in the hematocrit. AAS stimulate the formation of red blood cells
(erythropoiesis), which increases the percentage of the blood consisting of red
blood cells: the hematocrit. A great excess of red blood cells is called
polycythemia. The clinical importance of the hematocrit is debated, but it's likely
that above 54%, the risk of blood vessel blocking blood clots (thrombosis) and
strokes increases to clinical levels. There is no efficient therapy against these
effects. Donating blood is popular among AAS users but will only temporarily
decrease the hematocrit by around 3%, which is generally insufficient to
chronically lower the hematocrit.

None of these effects are very acutely life-threatening. In fact, most AAS users will
probably not notice any effects from them. However, walking around with these side-
effects in the long run will significantly increase your risk of cardiovascular disease.

Psychiatric side-effects
Testosterone is well-known to improve the mood and libido of hypogonadal individuals.
However, in individuals with normal testosterone levels, RCTs find surprisingly few -
often no - psychological effects of testosterone supplementation in most individuals.
There is generally no change in mood, cognition or aggression during testosterone
supplementation in healthy men [2, 3, 4]. The only somewhat consistent psychological
effect of testosterone is on libido: many AAS, including testosterone, increase libido [2,
3].

34

Testosterone is often said to cause aggression, but this is a major oversimplification and
largely a myth. Even across individuals in studies, meta-analyses show only a weakly
positive relationship between testosterone levels and aggression, and almost
exclusively in men, without evidence of causality. For example, being raised around
violence may raise testosterone levels as well as violent behavior, whereas being
around children can reduce both testosterone and violence, causing a between-
individual relationship between violence and testosterone that's mediated by the
environment, not a causal effect of testosterone. However, a small minority of
individuals taking AAS become manic and aggressive. Cross-sectional research has
linked multiple psychiatric problems with AAS use. This suggests AAS themselves, at
least the safer ones, do not readily cause psychiatric problems in most people, but
people with psychiatric problems may be particularly drawn to AAS use, resulting in
exacerbation of their symptoms. Thus, there is an ounce of truth to the concept of 'roid
rage', but it's not because androgens inherently turn sane individuals into violent
psychopaths.

Acne
AAS stimulate sebum production in the skin and a favorable environment on the skin for
certain acne-causing bacteria, making acne more common. Acne is particularly
common on the shoulders and back ('bacne'). See the course's Beauty module for more
details.

Hepatotoxicity
AAS that are c-17α-alkylated are hepatotoxic: they significantly stress the liver. For
more details, see the section on orals vs. injectables.

35

Prostate cancer
Prostate cancer is often reported as a potential side-effect of AAS use, but there is no
evidence that AAS use increases prostate cancer risk and androgen levels do not
correlate with prostate cancer incidence. AAS do increase prostate size quite reliably.
This is normally not a medical concern but can in some cases develop into benign
prostate hypertrophy (BPH). To be safe, it's advisable for AAS users to check prostate-
specific antigen (PSA) levels as part of their routine bloodwork.

Virilization
AAS, in particular DHT, have various androgenic or virilizing ('man making') effects. On
the scalp, they can cause hair loss due to androgenic alopecia: see the Beauty module
for more details. In the rest of the body, AAS can cause increased hair growth. In
women, this can cause hirsutism: hair growth in areas where women don't normally
have much hair, such as the mustache area. Women may also experience clitoral
enlargement, breast atrophy, voice hoarsening and deepening (dysphonia) and
menstrual irregularities if not complete amenorrhea. Many of these virilizations are
permanent once the new tissues have been fully formed, so women should be
monitoring their symptoms closely if they wish to avoid them. For example, voice
changes are often preceded by hoarseness and throat ache. The only way to prevent
exacerbation is to reduce the AAS dosage. Up to 5 mg per day of AAS generally pose
minimal risks of long-term virilization and no major acute side-effects, whereas over 50
mg per starts posing some acute risks even with a relatively safe AAS like
oxymetholone [2].

Gynecomastia

36

The formation of breast tissue in men, gynecomastia ('gyno'), is commonly feared by
men taking AAS, but there is very little risk when AAS are properly used. Gyno is not an
inevitable result of high estrogen levels. Gyno is primarily the result of a high ratio of
estrogenic to androgenic activity in breast tissue, causing growth of the breast glands as
typically occurs in women during puberty. The vital role of the ratio of androgens to
estrogens rather than merely absolute estrogen levels is why women on AAS often
experience breast atrophy rather than growth: there is more androgenic than estrogen
activity. In men, the ratio of androgens to estrogens should not become imbalanced by
AAS that don't have estrogenic, progestogenic or prolactin activity, unless they already
had excess aromatization to begin with. Excess aromatization is a common issue for
overweight men and indeed it's mostly overweight men that experience gyno on AAS.

Gyno can be effectively suppressed by selective estrogen receptor modulators
(SERMs), preferably tamoxifen citrate. These block estrogen receptors specifically in
breast tissue but not everywhere, preserving some of the beneficial effects of estrogens,
such as that of raising HDL-cholesterol levels. Any previously formed hard breast tissue
may be permanent, however, unless surgically removed. Fortunately, it's easy to detect
the onset of gynecomastia and suppress it before it becomes permanent, as it starts
with extremely sensitive and painful nipples (gynecodynea), followed by swelling of the
nipple area (lipomastia), fat deposition and only afterwards fibrous tissue formation of
the glands.

The use of aromatase inhibitors (AIs) can also be used to increase the ratio of
androgens to estrogens if the AAS in question can aromatize, but AIs are generally not
preferred in lean individuals, as eliminating estrogens increases androgenic side-effects
and SERMs tend to be more effective at reducing gynecomastia than AIs.

37

Programming for enhanced trainees
If you have clients on AAS, you should be familiar with how to program for them. In
general, all programming strategies that are effective in natural trainees are also
effective in AAS users and you won't go terribly wrong by ignoring their AAS use
altogether. However, the following program parameters require special considerations
for AAS users.

Protein intake
Due to the massive increase in muscle protein synthesis levels, protein requirements
most likely increase in AAS users. See the protein module for details.

Fat intake
Without a need for natural testosterone production, there is also less need for dietary
fat. Conversely, the significant increase in work capacity, glycogen storage and
tolerable training volume of AAS users might necessitate higher carbohydrate intakes to
maximize performance. As a rule of thumb, dietary fat intake can be set to the lower end
of the optimal range of natural trainees: 20% of energy intake is a good general
guideline. Saturated fat presents extra risks and no longer any benefits, so saturated fat
intake should be minimized and most fat intake should come from sources rich omega-3
fatty acids or mono-unsaturated fatty acids.

38

Energy balance
Since AAS massively increase muscle growth but do very little to aid fat loss, AAS
cycles should generally be used during bulking phases and these should be aggressive
to make the most out of the available time on AAS. It's generally safe to start the energy
surplus at 10% and then aggressively push up the calories as long as possible without
seeing excess fat gain. A well-designed AAS cycle can result in a kilo of lean body
mass gain per week, so the bulking energy intakes of AAS users should be
considerably higher than those of natural trainees.

Progress measurement
Measuring body composition changes in AAS users is much more challenging than in
natural trainees, especially if aromatizing AAS are used. For completely non-
aromatizing AAS, the same guidelines as for natural trainees can be used, although any
imaging scans, in particular BIA scans, become significantly less reliable. For
aromatizing or estrogenic AAS, circumference measurements are also of very limited
use, as it's impossible to distinguish between fluid retention, fat gain and muscle growth,
at least during the first few months until an equilibrium has been reached for hydration
status. This leaves skinfold calipers as the measurement tool of choice for most AAS
users.

Training volume
AAS users can likely tolerate a higher training volume than natural trainees. However,
there is no data on this. If you use the course's training volume calculator, the training
volume will automatically go up during bulking phases in line with the energy balance
surplus. This likely represents a good guideline to follow.

39

after the cycle return to baseline within several weeks or at worst months.

The data are compiled from the Mayo Clinic's publications and a survey of 120
laboratories in the US, which are in turn based on many scientific publications. If you
have bloodwork done and a different reference range is provided, always use that
instead. The lab equipment and calibration method can affect the measurements and
thus the reference range considerably.

41

Secondly, for optimal wellbeing in men, the higher reference range of 83-257 ng/dL
free/bioavailable testosterone may be preferable, as this is the range for men in their
20s.

42

Hypogonadism screening
For the testing of low testosterone (hypogonadism), free/bioavailable testosterone*** is
generally the primary and determining criterion for the diagnosis. The other hormones
levels are generally a secondary source of information and are more related to the
cause than the diagnosis. Even if high SHBG or progesterone is the cause of the low
testosterone levels, TRT will itself decrease their levels and increase free testosterone
levels.

For otherwise healthy individuals already living a lifestyle as recommended in this
course, a diagnosis of hypogonadism often has testosterone replacement therapy (TRT)
as the only long-term solution, but the following should always be evaluated first.
• Adequate sleep
• Sufficient intake of dietary fat, cholesterol, zinc, boron, magnesium and calcium
• A healthy amount of exercise resulting in strength development

Health screening
In addition to testing sex hormone levels pre- and post-cycle to assess the recovery of
natural testosterone production, it is highly advisable to perform on-cycle bloodwork to
assess cardiovascular health. Most side-effects of moderate testosterone-only cycles
not acutely dangerous, but the following biomarkers have been found to routinely go out
of the normal range when administering supraphysiological amounts of testosterone.
• HDL-cholesterol should ideally remain at least 25% as high as total non-HDL
cholesterol. HDL-cholesterol should also ideally stay within the physiological
range. Low HDL-cholesterol is not an immediate clinical concern, but it is
certainly a long-term cardiovascular health risk. LDL-cholesterol, or preferably
the ApoB particle number, should not rise to acutely concerning levels.
• The hematocrit, the blood volume percentage of red blood cells, should ideally
stay below 50% and absolutely below 60% to keep the blood from becoming too

43

thick and prevent thrombosis and blood clots, though it is debatable if a high
hematocrit itself is a risk factor when blood pressure and other cardiovascular
health markers are normal [2].
• Systolic and diastolic blood pressure should be monitored, using cuffs suitable
for individuals with muscular arms.
• Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels should be monitored as a marker of
prostate health.
• Liver enzymes, in particular GGT, should be monitored during the use of any 17-
alpha-alkylated AAS or other oral medications.

44

Medical injection guidelines
If someone's going to use injectables, it's paramount they know how inject themselves
in the most medically responsible fashion, or that they can instruct someone to inject
them. The following guide that you can send to your clients contains instructions for this.

➢ Guide
How to perform a medical injection

45

How many people use AAS?
Those in the know in the fitness industry are aware steroid use is rampant. Since very
few users are understandably unwilling to publicly admit to illegal drug usage, it can be
difficult to get an idea of how many people exactly are on gear. The best data we have
comes from anonymous scientific surveys in the US [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Based on these,
below are the percentages of male AAS users in the United States.
•
2.7% of all middle school students (ages 9 to 13)
•
4.7% of all 7th to 12th graders (ages 12 to 17)
•
6.6% of all 12th graders (age 17)
•
54% of competitive bodybuilders
•
Over 90% of professional bodybuilders
It's safe to say these are underestimations.
•
Most of these estimates were based on self-reporting.
•
The users in several of these studies themselves thought the actual incidence of
drug use was higher than reported in the study.
•
Steroid use was on the rise in these studies and all of this research is over 14
years old.
headings: below are the percentages of male AAS users in the United States.

pages.text:
46

Sourcing
This course will never contain information on where to find or purchase PEDs, but we
do feel obligated to provide some directions for those that will do so on their own. PEDs
can be purchased from 2 main sources: pharmacies and underground labs (UGLs).
Pharmacies have strict quality control, so you can expect their products to be 'pharma
grade'. UGLs are generally underground, black-market manufacturers, so they have no
quality control. Unsurprisingly, UGL products are of highly dubious quality. A 2022
meta-analysis found that only about a third of UGL AAS are approximately pharma
grade. One more third has the labeled ingredients but is typically underdosed, although
sometimes also overdosed. The remaining third is counterfeit: adulterated, inert or not
what's on the label. "These drugs could contain no active ingredient, or in another
amount than that labeled, a wrong active ingredient, as well as not all or more active
ingredients than were labeled." Even if it's more expensive, it's much safer to buy
pharma products than UGL products. If you buy from an UGL, make sure it has positive
reviews and there are independent lab analyses available of their products.

47

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do the following.
• Explain the process of sex hormone production in men and women.
• Aid individuals set on using AAS (legally) do so in a medically responsible
manner.
• Explain why SARMs are not a risk-free way to use anabolics.
• Read someone's bloodwork pre- and post-AAS cycle and conclude if there's
damage to natural hormone production.
• Explain how AAS benefit muscle growth and strength development acutely as
well as chronically.

Note: This module is not tested on the exam, except the first section on how your body
produces sex hormones, but we've still provided learning objectives and a mock exam
to enhance the learning experience for those interested in this module.

When most people talk about steroids, they're actually referring to performance
enhancing drugs (PEDs) or anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS). Vitamin D, for
example, is also a steroid hormone, as are various medications without any muscle
building effects. There are also several other PEDs used by bodybuilders with the goal
to increase muscle growth or lose fat, such as growth hormone, insulin and thyroid
hormone. That most people don't even properly know what the terms mean illustrates
the lack of scientific knowledge on the topic. In this module of the course, we'll ignore
the taboo and discuss in scientific terms what 'steroids' do to the human body. This
topic is not about teaching you how to use steroids but rather inform you about the risks
and benefits from a medical point of view so that you can properly inform your clients
and potentially help mitigate the risks.

➢ Lecture
The costs and benefits of using androgenic-anabolic steroids (AAS)

➢ Case study
Deciding whether or not to use AAS


--- Page 4 ---
4

How your body produces sex hormones
Before looking at the effects of supplementing exogenous steroids, it helps to
understand how the human body produces its own steroids and what they do. Sex
hormone production differs considerably between the sexes and we'll look at men first,
because it's easy to understand how it works in women once you understand the
process in men.

Men
The hypothalamus in our brain is basically the master regulator of hormone levels. It
releases gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), which stimulates the pituitary gland
in the brain to release luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle stimulating hormone (FSH).
LH increases steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) in the Leydig cells of the
testicles. The testicles then start producing androgens, literally 'man making hormones',
notably testosterone. Almost all (95%) testosterone in men is produced by the testes,
but the adrenal glands also contribute about 5%. Steroids are produced from
cholesterol, so cholesterol is the fundamental building block of all sex hormones.
FSH also stimulates the testes but to produce sperm rather than testosterone.

Estrogen production in men occurs primarily via aromatization (conversion) of
androgens in adipose tissue (body fat) but also in bone and the testes. Testosterone is
converted to estradiol and androstenedione to estrone.


--- Page 5 ---
5

Steroid production in the male body.

The testes produce 4 - 9 mg of testosterone per day in the average healthy male.

Male testosterone production is controlled by a negative feedback loop that measures
the level of androgens and estrogens in the body. This needs to be at a certain level.
When that level is reached, the hypothalamus stops stimulating the pituitary to release
LH and FSH, which are then no longer there to activate the gonads, which then stop
producing testosterone and sperm. This testosterone is converted to estrogen via the
aromatase enzyme, primarily in adipose tissue (body fat).


--- Page 6 ---
6

When a man is on exogenous (meaning 'from outside of the body') androgenic-anabolic
steroids (AAS), these are registered by the hypothalamus just as if they were the body's
own testosterone and thus natural production is shut down: there are already enough
androgens in circulation as far as the body goes, so further production isn't needed.
Natural production of testosterone is fully suppressed as soon as exogenous dosages
exceed physiological production levels, so in practice, you can assume any use of AAS
fully shuts down the testes.

The negative feedback loop regulation of testosterone production in men. Endogenous
testosterone and exogenous AAS have the same suppressive effect on the
hypothalamus and pituitary. Adapted from Source

The suppression of natural production is undesirable, as it results in infertility, atrophy of
the testes ('raisin nuts') and over time the possibility of complete cell death. This would


--- Page 7 ---
7

render someone infertile and hypogonadal, incapable of producing enough testosterone
and consequently estrogen for normal physiological functioning.

Women
Women produce only 5-10% as much testosterone as men, but androgens are
important for female functioning just like estrogens are important for male functioning.
Sex hormone production in women starts in the brain just like in men. The
hypothalamus produces GnRH, which stimulates the pituitary to release LH and FSH.

Since women don't have testicles, things obviously differ from here on out. LH binds not
to Leydig cells in the testes but to Theca cells in the ovaries, which then produce
androgens. Whereas men are practically entirely reliant on their testes to produce
testosterone, women derive only about half of their testosterone from the ovaries. The
other half comes indirectly from the adrenal gland, which lies on top of your kidneys.
The adrenals are also under control of the pituitary via release of adrenocorticotropic
hormone (ACTH) and together form the hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. The
adrenal cortex produces androgen precursor hormones, namely DHEA sulfate
(DHEAS), DHEA and androstenedione. These weaker androgens are converted to
testosterone in their target tissues.


--- Page 8 ---
8

Sex hormone production in the adrenal gland. Source

Women produce estrogen via aromatization of testosterone in peripheral tissues,
particularly adipose tissue, just like in men, but the ovaries also directly produce a lot of
estrogen. In the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle, the corpus luteum also produces
estrogen directly.


--- Page 9 ---
9

Women do not have the same simple negative feedback loop of testosterone production
as men. There is negative feedback based on the level of estradiol and progesterone,
but it interacts with the menstrual cycle phase in a complex manner, illustrated below. In
practice, most women can use AAS without experiencing shutdown of natural sex
hormone production. They will often, but not always, lose their menstrual cycle, but
there is relatively little risk of long-term infertility and sex hormone deficiency.

Natural hormone production in women.

During pregnancy, sex hormone levels can increase majorly. Even testosterone levels
can increase 3- to 4-fold.

Menopause & HRT
Women in their 40s or 50s go into menopause, a state of ovarian senescence. The
process begins around age 35 with full menopause - the complete loss of menstruation
- occurring at an average age of 51. As the ovaries stop functioning, sex hormone
production and fertility decrease majorly. Most women become completely infertile,


--- Page 10 ---
10

testosterone levels drop by roughly half and estrogen levels decrease from 15-350
pg/mL (wide variation across the menstrual cycle) to below 10 pg/mL. Postmenopausal
estrogen levels are actually on average lower than those of adult men (10-40 pg/mL).
These dramatic decreases in sex hormone levels result in a wide range of side-effects,
including lower mental health and wellbeing, loss of libido and vaginal dryness, poorer
connective tissue strength and many more problems, as illustrated below.

Female hormone levels before and after menopause. Source


--- Page 11 ---
11

Symptoms of menopause. Source

Throughout most of human evolution, women would not live much past the age of
menopause, but in modern societies, women spend about a third of their lives post-
menopause. Therefore, women undergoing menopause are recommended to consider
hormone replacement therapy (HRT) to bring their hormone levels back to their
previous levels, undoing many of the effects of menopause.
• HRT improves women's body composition and physical performance without
exercise and augments muscle growth and some measures of physical
performance with exercise.


--- Page 12 ---
12

• HRT likely reduces injury risk during strength training, as menopause causes
bone loss and increased susceptibility to muscle damage. Estrogen stimulates
collagen synthesis in connective tissues, notably tendons. Estrogen also reduces
connective tissue stiffness, which can be good or bad, depending on the context.
Reducing estrogen levels with aromatase inhibitors frequently causes joint pain
as a side-effect and AAS users with low estrogen levels also frequently
experience joint pain, anecdotally.
• HRT significantly improves skin and hair quality.
• Aside from the physical benefits, HRT's main benefit is arguably decreased
anxiety, decreased depression and a better overall mood.

HRT does not affect all-cause mortality, although it may increase the risk of some
cardiovascular problems (without a net increase in mortality). The risk of
cardiovascular complications may primarily result from progestogens, because
estrogen-only HRT has been found to improve cardiovascular health [2]. Still, some
progesterone is usually incorporated into HRT to reduce the risk of endometrial
cancer.

➢ Recommended reading for postmenopausal women
IMS recommendations on women's midlife health and menopause hormone therapy


--- Page 13 ---
13

AAS mechanism of action
AAS operate primarily via genomic effects: they bind to the androgen receptor, which
activates genetic pathways to encode new proteins. This heightened rate of protein
synthesis alters your body structure and functions, including large increases in muscle
protein synthesis with rapid muscle growth and strength development [2]. Since this is
an indirect and slow process, the effects of AAS continue to accumulate for months
during their use. This goes for muscle growth as well as the side-effects. The images
below illustrate AAS' mechanism of action and their effects in the body.

How androgens improve muscular development. Source


--- Page 14 ---
14

Testosterone's effects in relation to that of strength training. Source


--- Page 15 ---
15

A list of the performance enhancing effects of androgens. Source

AAS also have non-genomic effects [2]: effects that are not mediated by your genes.
Instead of entering into a muscle cell and instructing it to increase muscle protein
synthesis, testosterone can also act on receptors and calcium channels on the cell
membrane. These effects are far more rapid and direct, but they contribute considerably
less to neuromuscular development than the genomic effects. The non-genomic effects
seem to be mediated mostly via membrane receptors that affect calcium metabolism. In
muscle fibers, the presence of calcium triggers the reactions that make actin and
myosin form cross-bridges, which create muscle contractions. Altered calcium
metabolism appears to be the primary non-genomic mechanism by which AAS increase
muscle strength almost acutely.

Dose-response effects
The effect of AAS are strongly dose-dependent: the more AAS you have in your blood,
the greater the androgenic and anabolic effects and thus the more muscle you'll gain [2,
3]. This does-response effect is illustrated in the graph below.

Since the side-effects of AAS are also dose-dependent, many novice AAS users think
they can get away with 'using just a little bit', but this In the figure you can see there is
no positive effect on muscle growth until you give a man more than 50 mg per week of
testosterone. Doses below 100 mg/wk are essentially testosterone replacement therapy
(TRT). They do not increase your total blood testosterone levels. Because testosterone
levels are regulated via a negative feedback loop in men, up until this point you're just
replacing natural androgens with exogenous ones. Only when you take higher doses,
do you reach supraphysiological androgen levels and then you can achieve increased
muscle growth. Using a TRT-like dose this thus takes all the risks of shutting down your
natural production without anything to show for it. For an AAS cycle, any dose below
300 mg/wk is arguably futile.

Doses of up to 600 mg/wk of testosterone have been relatively well-documented [2, 3,
4] and do not cause high acute health hazards, although they certainly cause clinical
side-effects, especially in the elderly. There is scant research on higher doses, so 600

contrast, many professional bodybuilders have been reported to take around 2000
mg/wk of AAS, in addition to various other PEDs.

Choice of AAS
Bro steroid lore bases much of its knowledge on which AAS to use on their androgenic-
anabolic ratios. The idea is that you want AAS with very anabolic effects but little
androgenic effects: maximum muscle growth with as little of the side-effects as possible.
However, the AA ratios commonly cited are based on research in rodents. We know
that in humans, AAS operate principally via the androgen receptor, which causes both
the anabolic and the androgenic effects. It's thus impossible for any AAS to have
anabolic effects without androgenic effects. The two go hand in hand. Accordingly,
research finds that on a mg per mg basis, most AAS increase lean body mass to the
same extent: see the figure below.


--- Page 18 ---
18

The dose-response effect of various AAS is very similar on a mg per mg basis. Source

Other research supports that trenbolone acetate, commonly abbreviated to tren, is no
more anabolic or androgenic than DHT or testosterone in human cells (but it does have
unique other effects not mediated by the AR), and 300 mg 19-nortestosterone
decanoate, commonly abbreviated to deca, even appears to be slightly weaker than 300
mg testosterone, in contrast to what you'd expect based on their AA ratios in rodents.
Furthermore, adding a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor to a testosterone cycle, which
decreases the level of DHT while increasing testosterone, does not affect gains in lean
body mass, indicating DHT and testosterone have similar anabolic effects.


--- Page 19 ---
19

The principal determinant of any AAS cycle or stack is thus simply the total weekly mg
of AAS, analogous to the effects of training volume on muscle growth (i.e. total sets per
muscle) and energy intake for fat loss (total kcal per week). With this in mind, the most
important consideration of which AAS to use should generally not be which is strongest
but which is safest. In this sense, testosterone should always be considered first,
because it's a natural substance for the human body and it's by far the most extensively
researched AAS. The popular saying in bodybuilding circles that 'test is best' rings true
in many cases. However, testosterone may not always be appropriate for an individual's
goals, so let's discuss a few notable factors that differentiate AAS. One major
determinant of the safety of an AAS is whether it's 17α-alkylated.

Orals vs. injectables
Raw hormones are not absorbed well by the body. They are typically broken down
before they make it to the bloodstream, in particular by the liver. Chemists have found a
way around this with a process called c-17α-alkylation in order to make AAS orally
consumable. All oral AAS, except some rather ineffective forms of oral testosterone,
mesterolone (commonly known under the brand name Proviron) and metenolone
acetate (commonly known under the brand name Primobolan) are 17α-alkylated,
whereas almost no injectable AAS are 17-alpha-alkylated. Stanozolol, commonly known
under the brand name Winstrol, is the only know-worthy exception of an AAS that is
commonly injected yet still is hepatotoxic and can be consumed orally as well. Making
the AAS resistant to the liver's attempts at breaking it down makes it orally effective, but
it also puts a major strain on the liver, resulting in many additional side-effects. Since
the liver plays a crucial role in human metabolism and detoxification of the body, the
side-effect profile of oral AAS is often dramatically worse than that of injectables at
higher doses. The worst cases of strange pathologies, hospitalizations and deaths from
AAS use almost always included oral AAS. Since there is very little orals can do that
injectables can't, oral AAS should generally be avoided by men. Women can achieve


--- Page 20 ---
20

significant gains from much lower doses of AAS, so orals are comparatively safer to use
for them up to a point.

Half-lives
Largely depending on which ester an AAS is bound to, AAS vary significantly in their
terminal half-lives: the time it takes for the body to eliminate half of the AAS from the
bloodstream after its peak. The half-life does not fundamentally change the effects of
the AAS, but having major fluctuations in blood concentrations of the AAS can increase
its side-effects, as all women with a menstrual cycle can attest to. Acne is particularly
common during large changes in blood AAS levels. The fluctuations arising from AAS
with different half-lives are illustrated below, as are the half-lives of common
testosterone preparations.

Frontloading
Another strategy to prevent excessive fluctuations in AAS levels is frontloading. If you
look at the graph above of weekly testosterone enanthate injections, you'll see the
testosterone blood concentration accumulates slowly over time, because not all the
testosterone from the previous injection is eliminated by the time of the next dose. It
then takes around 5 weeks to reach peak testosterone concentration. That's 5 weeks of
constantly changing testosterone concentrations below the target level. Frontloading is
a method to get to target level from the first injection onward. Based on an AAS's half-
life, we can calculate how much AAS is needed for the initial injection to immediately
reach equilibrium levels. During the start of AAS usage or when changing the dose, the

Aromatization
Another considerable difference between AAS is whether they aromatize - convert to
estrogen - in our body or not. Aromatization in men preserves the natural ratio of
testosterone to estrogen in the body. Estrogen helps counteract androgenic side-
effects, such as the decrease in HDL-cholesterol, and as you learned, estrogen itself
also has many desirable anti-catabolic and even anabolic effects, though they're not as
potent as testosterone's. However, estrogen also causes considerable sodium and
water retention. Trainees on high doses of testosterone will often experience edema
and a puffy appearance including 'moon face', so they will not see much muscle
definition, especially in the abdominal region, as it's covered by a thick layer of
subcutaneous fluid. A significant portion of the increases in lean body mass during a
testosterone cycle - many kilos in the case of a heavyweight bodybuilder - are
essentially just water, which explains why strength does not increase nearly as much as
lean body mass during a testosterone cycle.

To counteract this aesthetic problem, bodybuilders often use non-aromatizing AAS so
that they get more muscular without getting bloated. The last month(s) before a non-
natural bodybuilding show, non-aromatizing AAS are essentially a must to look lean on
stage. The use of aromatizing vs. non-aromatizing AAS thus presents an inevitable
compromise between aesthetics and androgenic side-effects.

AAS for women
The myth of androgenic to anabolic ratios has given rise to the idea of AAS for women
in bro bodybuilding culture. The truth is, there is no such thing as an AAS that will not
cause virilizing side-effects in women. Oxandrolone is popular with women because it
does not aromatize, but the only reason it's known as less virilizing is because it is


--- Page 24 ---
24

typically used in low doses, as it's generally incredibly expensive. Physiologically,
however, 'test is best' likely also applies to women, because virtually all of estrogen's
effects are desirable in women, except often its water retention. The main problem with
testosterone use in women is that it's difficult to micro-dose. Most testosterone
preparations are made with men in mind, so the doses are far too high for women.
Moderate AAS use for women that should not cause major virilization is around 35-105
mg per week (5-15 mg per day). Anything over 350 mg per week (50 mg per day) is
hard to tolerate for more than a few weeks or months without noticeable virilization. The
typically highest tolerable dose in women is thus around the lowest effective dose for a
cycle in men.

An unpopular but anecdotally effective alternative to testosterone in women is
oxymetholone. Oxymetholone causes estrogenic side-effects such as gynecomastia
and major water retention, suggesting it has direct estrogenic effects despite not
aromatizing. Like virtually all oral AAS, it will strain the liver, but even 100 mg per day
for 3 months has been reported to be reasonably well-tolerated in many women. The
estrogenic activity may offset some of the androgenic side-effects.

SARMs
Selective androgen receptor modulators (SARMs) are supposed to be the future of
steroids. Their intent is to activate the androgen receptor like AAS do without being
susceptible to human enzymes. Without any metabolic byproducts, the SARM should
have fewer side-effects than AAS and not affect your natural anabolic hormone
production as much.

One of the few relevant studies we have on SARMs is Dalton et al. (2011). The
researchers investigated the effects of GTx-024 AKA enobosarm AKA ostarine AKA
MK-2866 in healthy elderly men and post-menopausal women. After 12 weeks, the
results as presented in the abstract seem promising: "GTx-024 treatment resulted in
dose-dependent increases in total lean body mass that were statistically significant and


--- Page 25 ---
25

clinically meaningful. There were also significant improvements in physical function
(P = 0.013, 3 mg vs. placebo) and insulin resistance. The incidence of adverse events
was similar between treatment groups." The authors worked for the company GTx that
produces SARMs, however, and a closer inspection reveals more sobering findings.

While the authors present an impressive graph showing relative changes in lean body
mass (see below), only the highest dosage of 3 mg per day resulted in a significant
increase in lean body mass and this was a mere 1.3 kg. They were also the only group
to lose a significant amount of fat, but this was only 0.6 kg.

Lack of gains is one thing, but the side-effects the authors conveniently omitted in the
abstract were serious.
• 'Good' HDL cholesterol decreased in dose-response fashion with a considerable
28% drop in the highest dosage group, i.e. the more of the SARM they took, the
worse their cholesterol levels got.
• Natural hormone production was negatively affected. Testosterone levels in the
men decreased in dose-response fashion with a 43% decrease in the highest
dosage group. Interestingly, even the women showed impaired hormone
functioning. The highest dosage group experienced a decrease in FSH and LH


--- Page 26 ---
26

levels. All groups experienced a dose-response decrease in estradiol production
with a 34% decrease in the highest dosage group.

It's also worth noting one subject had to be removed from the study because his ALT
liver enzyme values were too high. 21% of subjects in the highest dosage group
experienced an increase in ALT, suggesting liver damage.

The only other relevant SARM study we have is Basaria et al. (2013). This is again an
industry sponsored study. 76 Healthy men took 0.1, 0.3 or 1.0 mg per day of the SARM
LGD-4033 for 21 days. The results are similar to those of Dalton et al. There was a
dose-response increase in lean body mass of 1.2 kg in the highest dosage group and a
concurrent decrease in testosterone and HDL levels. At the highest dosage, FSH and
free testosterone were also negatively affected. Interestingly, the SARM did not affect
strength or physical performance markers. Fasting muscle protein synthesis levels also
did not increase. These findings could mean the increase in lean body mass was mostly
water or other tissue than muscle, similar to growth hormone.

So are SARMs the magic pill? The data we have indicate they function very similarly to
(oral) AAS. Even at very modest dosages the side-effects are already significant; for
women the side-effects are even worse than AAS it seems. Research in rats found that
the SARM S-23 decreased natural testosterone production so much it's of better use as
a male contraceptive than a doping drug. There's no telling what havoc SARMs will
wreak on your body at dosages that are comparably potent to an AAS cycle.

Moreover, SARMs also suffer the same problem as AAS: if you buy SARMs from a non-
pharmaceutical grade source, there is a good chance the product is impure.

In short, while the theory behind SARMs is nice, there's little research to support they
actually work and no evidence that they cause fewer side-effects than AAS at


--- Page 27 ---
27

comparably effective dosages. The use of SARMs cannot be considered medically
responsible at this point.

Side-effects of AAS cycles
AAS use comes with multiple well-established side-effects [2]. Virtually no AAS users
are free from side-effects. Below we will discuss the most common and notable side-
effects.

Hypogonadism
As discussed, the use of external androgens will suppress natural androgen production
in men, resulting in hypogonadism. Without the use of ancillaries to mitigate the
hypogonadism and improve its recovery, it typically takes months for the testes to fully
recover after AAS use and natural testosterone production may still not reach pre-use
levels. To prevent hypogonadism during AAS use, hCG can and generally should be
used.

hCG to prevent testicular shutdown
Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is a hormone produced by a woman's placenta
during pregnancy. It's measured by many urinary pregnancy tests. As it turns out, hCG
is an analog of LH, so injecting hCG in men stimulates the testes to produce
testosterone just like LH. As such, hCG use maintains fertility and testicular functioning
during AAS use [2, 3]. However, since hCG does not mimic the effects of FSH, which is
needed for maximum spermatogenesis, hCG maintains the quality but not the full
quantity of sperm on-cycle. hCG also stimulates the production of all androgens,
estradiol and progesterone.

In contrast to conventional practice, which is based on women, the dosage requirement
for men is very low. It takes an injection dosage of only 250-500 IUs hCG every other
day to fully maintain testicular functioning. 500 IU twice a week comes close to fully
maintaining testicular functioning as well. Higher dosages mainly stimulate more


--- Page 29 ---
29

estradiol production with little additional effect on testicular functioning [2]: see the
graphs below.
